title: Bloomer
subtitle: The split-top oval British white loaf
excerpt: A tapered oval white loaf with five diagonal slashes across the top and a crackling, golden crust. Simple to shape, forgiving to bake, and one of the most satisfying slicing breads in the British repertoire.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, bloomer-style oval bread documented as a common bakers' shape. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the five-slash pattern and the tapered oval form are recorded as the traditional British baker's mark for this loaf.

content.text: The bloomer is a baker's shape, not a tin loaf. It proves free-form on a tray, so the crust forms all the way round. The five diagonal slashes open up during baking, giving the loaf its characteristic scored face and allowing the crumb to expand without tearing at the sides. A straightforward bake for a beginner: one dough, one shape, no specialist tin.
Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more. An oven thermometer ensures the crust sets properly. A pale, thick crust is almost always a sign the oven was running cool.

Mix {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, and {{salt-table}} in a large bowl. Add the {{water}} and bring together to a shaggy dough. Turn out and knead for 10 minutes until smooth, elastic, and passing the
: stretch a small piece between your fingers. It should thin to a near-translucent sheet without tearing.
Place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and leave in a warm spot until doubled, about 1 hour. The time varies with temperature; the visual cue is what matters.

Gently flatten the dough into a rough rectangle. Fold the long sides into the centre, then roll tightly from one short end into a fat oval, tapering the ends with your palms so it roughly narrows toward both tips. Place seam-side down on a lined baking tray. Cover with oiled cling film.
Leave in a warm place for 45 minutes until noticeably puffed and the indent left by a floured finger springs back slowly. Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan) with a roasting tray on the bottom shelf.

Dust the loaf generously with flour. Using a sharp knife held at 45 degrees, make five diagonal slashes across the top, evenly spaced, each about 1 cm deep. Pour 200 ml cold water into the hot roasting tray. Slide the baking tray onto the middle shelf and bake for 15 minutes. Remove the water tray, drop the temperature to 190°C (170°C fan), and bake for a further 20 minutes. The crust should be a deep, even brown; the loaf should sound hollow when tapped underneath; an instant-read thermometer through the base should read 96°C. Rest on a wire rack for 30 minutes before cutting.
